nativeRange.habitat: Rocky sections of large river systems; fast-flowing waters with boulder and rocky substrates, hiding among crevices and caves during the day

decorPreferences: large smooth boulders and rock formations with caves
PVC pipes or ceramic tubes as hiding spots
dim or subdued lighting to encourage daytime activity
sturdy rooted plants (Java fern, Anubias on rocks/wood)

diet.notes: Accepts a wide variety of foods: high-quality sinking pellets and wafers (catfish pellets, algae wafers) form the dietary staple. Supplement with frozen or live foods — bloodworms, earthworms, brine shrimp, and prawn. Also grazes on algae and biofilm. Feed after lights-out to accommodate nocturnal habits. Avoid vitamin C-deficient diets and overfeeding protein.

goodWith: other large catfish of different genera
avoidWith: small fish that may be eaten or harassed
other Synodontis of similar or smaller size (territorial aggression)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo of Synodontis angelicus adult showing characteristic spotted pattern.

summary: A striking large Synodontis catfish from the Congo Basin, famous for its bold white-spotted juvenile pattern that fades to smaller spots on a darker base with age. Nocturnal and secretive, it requires ample caves and rockwork. Copper-sensitive; intermediate care due to adult size (20–25 cm) and territorial tendencies.
careNotes:
The Polka Dot Squeaker (Synodontis angelicus) is one of the most visually dramatic of all freshwater catfishes, and its common name reflects two of its most notable traits: the vivid polka-dot pattern of juveniles and the ability to produce audible stridulation sounds by locking its pectoral spine against its shoulder girdle — a characteristic shared by Mochokidae. Juveniles display a jet-black body adorned with large, bright white or cream spots that command attention in the trade; adults retain spotting but the spots become smaller and more numerous against a dark brownish or slate-grey base. This juvenile-to-adult colour transition (juvenileColorPhase=true) means juveniles are sometimes sold without buyers realising the adult appearance differs markedly from the fish they purchased.

A tank of at least 300 L with a 150 cm footprint is recommended to accommodate adults. The species is strongly nocturnal and spends daylight hours wedged in rocky crevices or caves. Providing multiple large caves, smooth boulders, and PVC hides is essential; without adequate shelter the fish will be chronically stressed. Lighting should be dim or well-shaded. Feed after lights-out with sinking pellets, catfish wafers, and occasional meaty foods such as frozen bloodworms, earthworms, or prawn.

Synodontis angelicus originates from fast-flowing rocky sections of the Congo River Basin. Water should be soft to moderately hard (gH 4–15), slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5), and well-oxygenated with moderate to strong flow. Temperature 22–26°C is appropriate. Like all catfishes with few or no scales, S. angelicus is highly sensitive to copper-based medications (formalin-copper treatments for ich, etc.); salt-free, copper-free treatments must always be used.

Territoriality can be pronounced, especially toward conspecifics. Adults are best kept singly or in very large aquaria where territories can be established. Good tankmates include large, robust cichlids and barbs of comparable size that inhabit the upper or mid water column. Small fish will be consumed. The species is generally peaceful with dissimilar tankmates too large to swallow but may nip fins of slow-moving companions.
breedingNotes: Breeding S. angelicus in home aquaria is essentially undocumented. In the wild the species is thought to spawn seasonally in river floods. No reliable captive breeding protocol has been established; the species is considered "not in captivity" for practical aquarist purposes. Distinguishing sexes is difficult; females may be plumper when gravid. Commercial specimens are wild-caught from the Congo Basin.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Synodontis angelicus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 25 cm), water parameters (pH 6.0–7.5, temperature 22–26°C, hardness 36–215 ppm), native range (Congo Basin, DRC, Republic of Congo, Cameroon), habitat (fast-flowing rocky sections), diet (omnivore, sinking pellets, frozen foods), behaviour (nocturnal, stridulation), tankmate guidance, and breeding information (not bred in captivity).

additional.name: FishBase — Synodontis angelicus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Mochokidae, order Siluriformes), adult size (to 25.0 cm TL), distribution (Congo River Basin, DRC, Cameroon), habitat (demersal, freshwater), temperature range. Confirmed copper sensitivity typical of scaleless catfishes.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Synodontis Catfish Care
additional.notes: General Synodontis care guide. Confirmed omnivore diet (sinking pellets, frozen bloodworms, blanched vegetables), nocturnal behaviour, cave/rockwork requirements, and copper sensitivity for the genus. Tank size guidance for larger Synodontis species consistent with 300 L recommendation.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Synodontis angelicus profile
additional.notes: Confirmed juvenile vs adult colour phase difference (large white spots on black juvenile fading to smaller spots on dark grey in adults), stridulation behaviour, adult size 20–25 cm, Congo Basin origin, territorial behaviour with conspecifics, and intermediate care rating.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Polka Dot Squeaker (Synodontis angelicus)
additional.notes: Supplementary source. Confirmed common names (Polka Dot Squeaker, Angelicus Cat, Angel Squeaker), stridulation via pectoral spine locking mechanism, nocturnal foraging habits, Congo River fast-water biotope preference, need for heavy cover and low light during the day, and diet breadth (carnivorous lean with omnivore acceptance of plant matter).

fish.breedingNotes: No established captive breeding protocol exists. Wild-caught specimens dominate the trade. Seasonal flood-triggered spawning is suspected in the wild; replication in aquaria has not been reliably achieved.
